stories.title: The Warden's Forge
stories.text:
Korgan Ironfoot had guarded the Deepvault forges for sixty winters, and in that time had never once struck a false blow. The vaults lay three miles beneath the Hearthlands, where stonecraft-workers hammered out weapons of legend and kept the old songs alive in stone and flame.

When the young thief Brinna descended the spiral stair with stolen blueprints and a demon's mark burned into her palm, Korgan knew at once what she was. The mark thrummed with hunger—a binding that would compel her to steal the Heartstone of Vulgrim, an artifact locked in the deepest forge chamber.

"I don't want this," Brinna whispered, showing him her burning hand.

Korgan set down his hammer. He'd heard tales of demons in the upper world, of compulsions that devoured free will. He studied the mark—ancient magic, cruel and timeless. "A demon's oath," he said. "Can't be broken by force. But a forge-oath..." He gripped her wrist and spoke in the stonecraft tongue, words that shook the stone itself. "By hammer and hearth, I bind you to my service. Speak your true name."

Brinna gasped as the demon's mark faded, replaced by Korgan's silver rune. The binding transferred.

"You'll work the forges with me," he said. "Seven years, and the oath dissolves. The demon can't claim you then—you'll belong to the stone and fire."

Brinna wept. For the first time in months, her hand was her own.

Korgan returned to his anvil and began to sing—the old song of protection, the one stonecraft-workers sang when they made weapons for heroes. Brinna, still trembling, picked up a hammer and learned the rhythm. Below them, the Heartstone burned safely in its chamber, guarded by something stronger than any theft: the unbreakable oath of a forge-master's binding.

stories.title: The Starfall Debt
stories.text:
In the weeks following the Crimson Convergence, rumors spread through the trade cities like spilled wine. The stars had changed. New constellations burned where old ones had dimmed. Merchants spoke in hushed tones of shortages—lamp oil was rationed, night-workers feared the altered dark, and astronomers were suddenly in demand and in danger, accused of causing the upheaval through forbidden workings.

Vex the Shade-Merchant heard the whispers first in Pelimor's shadow-markets. A figure had emerged from the Peak of Aethros—stripped of memory, stripped of self—and had been assigned to the Imperial archives by the Sovereign. Some said he was a broken thing. Others said he was a living conduit to the stars themselves.

Vex, cursed as she was, felt the weight of unfinished debts. The Celestial Binding had been performed. The price had been paid. But by whom? And what did that mean for the rest of them—the shadow-merchants, the oath-keepers, the collectors of impossible things?

She decided to find the Astronomer-Prince, not to steal from him, but to understand. In understanding, perhaps, she might glimpse the shape of her own curse's end.

stories.title: The Archivist's Vigil
stories.text:
Zareth sat in the Imperial archives, his fingers tracing columns of ledgers with methodical precision. He had no name for what he was doing, only that it felt necessary—the recording of days, the ordering of chaos. Other scribes moved around him like shadows. They whispered that he had been someone before, that the stars had spoken to him, that he had saved the world and lost himself in the doing.

He did not remember any of it.

But sometimes, late in the night-hours when oil-lamps guttered and the archives grew cold, Zareth would find himself staring upward through the high windows at the new constellations burning above. His hands would tremble. His throat would tighten. The knowledge that something vast and terrible and beautiful had been unmade and remade inside him hovered just beyond the reach of his stolen memory—a phantom weight, unbearable and permanent.

One evening, a woman in shadow-grey found him there. She said nothing, only watched. When she left, she took with her the certainty that Zareth's amnesia was not punishment, but price. And prices, she thought, could sometimes be renegotiated.

stories.title: The Shepherd of Dying Stars
stories.text: Across the Pallid Wastes, where few dared travel, an old hermit woman studied the new sky from the peak of a wind-scoured outcrop. Her name was forgotten; she preferred it that way. She had seen empires rise and fall. She had spoken with forest-keepers who remembered the before-time. And she knew, with the certainty that only comes from centuries of watching, that something had shifted in the cosmic order. The stars had been dying. Now they burned renewed. She traced the new constellations in ash and bone, reading the language of celestial arrangement. The working had been enormous—a ritual of such scope and sacrifice that it had scraped something essential from the caster. An oath sworn to all the stars themselves. A debt unpaid, merely deferred. The hermit began to walk. She had heard of an Astronomer-Prince in the Imperial archives. She had heard of the Sorcerer's journals buried in the Vaulted Tombs. She had heard whispers of a Shade-Merchant cursed to wander. These threads, she felt, would soon tangle together. And when they did, the next chapter of the cosmic cycle would begin to write itself.

stories.title: The Tax Collector's Reckoning
stories.text:
The Empire's coffers had begun to crack. Trade routes that had been reliable for decades were now marked by delays, shortages, and erratic behavior from merchants claiming they were adjusting to "changed celestial circumstances." Spice caravans reported that certain routes through the Scorched Reach were suddenly impassable—as if the land itself had shifted.

The Treasurer, ever meticulous, tasked his aide with investigating the disruption. She returned with troubling news: multiple merchants reported that their apprentices had gone mad, staring upward at the new stars and speaking in tongues. Three warehouses had caught fire under mysterious circumstances. And somewhere in the city, a shadow-merchant was moving through the markets asking questions about a memory-stripped figure in the archives.

The Treasurer understood leverage and information. Something vast had happened. Something that would reshape the world's commerce and power. He called for reports from every district, every trade post, every wealthy trader. If chaos was coming, he would map it first. Control would follow.

His aide, however, wondered if the Empire's architecture—built on certainty and order—could survive whatever the cosmos had begun to unfold.

stories.title: The Merfolk's Remembrance
stories.text:
In the depths of the Cerulean Trench, the sea-folk felt the change before surface-dwellers could name it. The tides had shifted. The currents remembered differently. And the memories encoded in water—ancient, sacred, older than kingdoms—sang of sacrifice and rebirth.

Commander Nerys called the elders to council. One spoke of an old oath, sworn before the Crimson Convergence had a name: a pact between the sea-folk and the land-dwellers that if the stars should fail, those who dwelt beneath would not survive the darkness. The pact was sealed. The stars had been renewed. Therefore, the debt was owed.

But to whom? And how would payment be demanded?

Nerys tasked her fastest swimmers to search the surface settlements, to listen in harbors and trade posts, to discover what working had been performed and what price had been extracted. She had heard rumors of an Astronomer-Prince and of magical exhaustion spreading through the land like a slow poison.

Whatever had happened above had echoes in the deep. The sea-folk would not be unprepared when those echoes became demands.

stories.title: The Warden's Sorrow
stories.text:
Ashara the Archer had held Thornholt for forty days against impossible odds. She had earned a reputation as a keeper of the impossible. So when rumors reached her of a vast working that had stripped an Astronomer-Prince of his memory to renew the stars, she understood something that others might miss: that sacrifice always echoed outward.

She began to notice strange behavior among her guards. Men and women who had endured the siege now found themselves weeping at unexpected moments, or staring into the darkness as if listening to voices. The old stone walls of Thornholt seemed to hum with a vibration that had not been present before.

Ashara consulted with the forest-keepers who dwelt in the borderlands. They told her that the earth itself was grieving—not for what was lost, but for what had been torn away so roughly. The Celestial Binding had worked, yes. But it had left a wound in the world that would take generations to heal.

She resolved to strengthen Thornholt not as a fortress of war, but as a sanctuary for those harmed by the working's aftermath. If the cosmos had been saved by breaking one man, then she would shelter the broken.

stories.title: The Sorcerer's Heir
stories.text:
In a spire hidden among the Blasted Lands, a young sorcerer discovered their master's final journal—a document left behind by The Sorcerer before his death. The entry was dated the night of the Crimson Convergence:

"I felt it. A working of such magnitude that it bent reality itself. Someone has sworn an oath that will reshape the cosmos. I do not know who. I do not know the cost. But I know that the ripples of such a working will spread outward for decades, touching every practitioner of the Art. The young ones especially—those who have not yet learned to shield themselves from celestial forces.

If you read this after my passing, know this: the price has been paid by someone else. Respect that sacrifice. Build something worthy of it."

The young sorcerer sat in silence, turning the pages. They had never known their master as a living being—only as records and journals and the echo of power that still clung to the spire's stones. Now they understood that the isolation they had always experienced was not punishment, but protection.

They began to study harder, preparing themselves for the moment when understanding and responsibility would collide. The cosmos had been renewed. Now it had to be maintained. And they would learn how.

stories.title: The Ghost of the Stellar Rite
stories.text: On the night of the Crimson Convergence, something emerged from between the stars—not a being, but a presence, a knot of consciousness that existed in the gaps between light and shadow. It had witnessed the Celestial Binding. It had felt the terrible extraction of memory from Zareth's mind. And it hungered to know why. This presence—neither demon nor ghost, but something older and stranger—began to move through the world, touching those who had been affected by the working. It whispered to shadow-merchants and sea-folk, to hermits and archers, to the grieving and the guilty. Vex the Shade-Merchant felt it first, since her own curse made her sensitive to forces that moved between states of being. She realized that the Celestial Binding had not simply renewed the stars—it had created a new kind of entity, born from Zareth's sacrificed memories. This thing was neither alive nor dead, neither fully present nor absent. It was the collective weight of what had been destroyed to save the world, and it was searching for wholeness.

stories.title: The Chorus of Altered Fates
stories.text:
Across the known world, soothsayers and seers reported a plague of conflicting visions. The future, which had seemed relatively fixed even hours before, had fractured into a thousand possible paths. Some prophets saw the world entering an age of stability and renewal. Others saw catastrophe approaching from the edges of the map. A few, the truly gifted ones, saw both simultaneously—as if the Crimson Convergence had split possibility itself into warring halves.

In Keth'moris, a young seer named Malla began to sketch the visions obsessively: images of a blank-faced archivist who held the key to either salvation or ruin. Of a shadow-merchant racing against time. Of sea-folk rising from the deep to make claims on the land. Of stars burning so brightly that they cast no shadows at all.

Malla brought her sketches to the remaining priests, to scholars and cartographers, to anyone who might help her understand. But no one could agree on what the visions meant. The Crimson Convergence had scrambled the language of prophecy itself.

So Malla did what desperate seers had always done: she began to travel, collecting other visions, other fragments, hoping that when enough pieces were gathered, the true shape of what was coming would finally reveal itself.
